and/or walkways; nor does it include excavation for a basement, footings, piers, or foundations or
the erection of temporary forms; nor does it include the installation on the property of accessory
buildings, such as garages or sheds not occupied as dwelling units or not part of the main
structure. For a substantial improvement, the actual start of construction means the first alteration
of any wall, ceiling, floor, or other structural part of a building, whether or not that alteration
affects the external dimensions of the building.
Structure: A walled and roofed building that is principally above ground; this includes a gas or
liquid storage tank or a manufactured home.
Substantial damage: Damage of any origin sustained by a structure whereby the cost of restoring
the structure to its before damaged condition would equal or exceed 50 percent of the market
value of the structure before the damage occurred.
Substantial improvement: Any reconstruction, rehabilitation, addition, or other improvement to a
structure, the total cost of which equals or exceeds 50 percent of the market value of the structure
before the "start of construction" of the improvement. This term includes structures which have
incurred "substantial damage," regardless of the actual repair work performed. The term does not,
however, include either:
Any project for improvement of a structure to correct existing violations of state
or local health, sanitary, or safety code specifications which have been identified by the local
code enforcement official and which are the minimum necessary to assure safe living conditions;
Or,
2.
Any alteration of a "historic structure," provided that the alteration will not
preclude the structure's continued designation as a "historic structure."
Variance: A grant of relief from the requirements of this ordinance which permits construction or
other uses of property in a manner that would otherwise be prohibited by this ordinance.
Violation: The failure of a structure or other development to be fully compliant with the
community's floodplain management regulations. A structure or other development without the
elevation certificate, other certifications, or other evidence of compliance required in this
ordinance is presumed to be in violation until such time as that documentation is provided.
Water surface elevation: The height, in relation to the National Geodetic Vertical Datum
(NGVD) of 1929, North American Vertical Datum (NAVD) of 1988, or other datum, of floods of
various magnitudes and frequencies in the floodplains of coastal or riverine areas.
Watercourse: A lake, river, creek, stream, wash, arroyo, channel or other topographic feature on
or over which waters flow at least periodically. Watercourse includes specifically designated
areas in which substantial flood damage may occur.
Zone A: No Base Flood Elevations determined.

Zone AE: Base Flood Elevations determined
Zone AH: Flood depths of 1 to 3 feet (usually areas of ponding); Base Flood Elevations
determined.
Zone AO: Flood depths of 1 to 3 feet (usually sheet flow on sloping terrain); average depths
determined. For areas of alluvial fan flooding, velocities also determined.
Zone AR: Special Flood Hazard Area formerly protected from the 1% annual chance flood by a
flood control system that was subsequently decertified. Zone AR indicates that the former flood
control system is being restored to provide protection from the 1% annual chance or greater.
flood.
Zone A99: Area to be protected from 1% annual chance flood by a Federal flood protection.
system under construction; no Base Flood Elevations determined.
Zone D: Areas in which flood hazards are undetermined, but possible.
Zone X (unshaded): Areas determined to be outside the 0.2% annual chance floodplain.
Zone X (shaded): Areas of 0.2% annual chance flood; areas of 1% annual chance flood with
average depths of less than 1 foot or with drainage areas less than 1 square mile; and areas
protected by levees from 1% annual chance flood

SECTION 3.0 GENERAL PROVISIONS 3.1 LANDS TO WHICH THIS ORDINANCE APPLIES This ordinance shall apply to all special flood hazard areas within the boundaries of Greenlee County except those incorporated cities and towns which have adopted a resolution in accordance with A.R.S. s48-3610. 3.2 BASIS FOR ESTABLISHING SPECIAL FLOOD HAZARD AREAS The special flood hazard areas identified by the Federal Emergency Management Agency in a scientific and engineering report entitled "The Flood Insurance Study (FIS) Number. 04011Cvoo0A for Greenlee County, Arizona and Incorporated Areas" dated September 28, 2007, and all subsequent amendments and/or revisions, are hereby adopted by reference and declared to be a part of this ordinance. This FIS and attendant mapping effective date September 28, 2007 is the minimum area of applicability of this ordinance and may be supplemented by studies for other areas which allow implementation of this ordinance and which are recommended to the Floodplain Board by the Floodplain Administrator. The Floodplain Board, within its area of jurisdiction, shall delineate (or may, by rule, require developers of land to. delineate) for areas where development is ongoing or imminent, and thereafter as development becomes imminent, floodplains consistent with the criteria developed by the Federal Emergency Management Agency and the Director of the Arizona Department of Water Resources. The FIS and FIRM panels are on file at Engineering Office, 253 Fifth Street, Clifton, Arizona 85533 or as established by the Floodplain Board. 3.3 COMPLIANCE All development of land, construction of residential, commercial or industrial structures, or future development within delineated floodplain areas is subject to the terms of this ordinance and other applicable regulations. 3.4 ABROGATION AND GREATER RESTRICTIONS This ordinance is not intended to repeal, abrogate or impair any existing easements, covenants or deed restrictions. However, where this ordinance and another ordinance, easement, covenant or deed restriction conflict or overlap, whichever imposes the more stringent restrictions shall prevail. 3.5 INTERPRETATION In the interpretation and application of this ordinance, all provisions shall be: 3.5.1 Considered as minimum requirements; 3.5.2 Liberally construed in favor of the governing body; and. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

3.6 DISCLAIMER OF LIABILITY The degree of flood protection required by this ordinance is considered reasonable for regulatory purposes and is based on scientific and engineering considerations. Larger floods can and will occur on rare occasions. Flood heights may be increased by man-made or natural causes. This ordinance does not imply that land outside the special flood hazard areas or uses permitted within such areas will be free from flooding or flood damages. This ordinance shall not create liability on the part of the Board of Directors of the Flood Control District of Greenlee County, any officer or employee thereof, the State of Arizona or the Federal Emergency Management Agency, for any flood damages that result from reliance on this ordinance or any administrative decision lawfully made hereunder.

3.7 STATUTORY EXCEPTIONS 3.7.1 In accordance with A.R.S. s 48-3609(H), unless expressly provided, this and any regulation adopted pursuant to this article do not affect:. 3.7.1.1 Existing legal uses of property or the right to continuation of such legal use. However, if a nonconforming use of land or a building or structure is discontinued for twelve months, or destroyed to the extent of 50 percent of its market value as determined by a competent appraiser, any further use shall comply with this article and regulations of Board of Directors of the Flood Control District of Greenlee County;. 3.7.2.2 Reasonable repair or alteration of property for the purposes for which the property was legally used on August 3, 1984, or on the date any regulations affecting such property takes effect, except that any alteration, addition or repair to a nonconforming building or. structure which would result in increasing its flood damage potential by 50 percent or more shall be either floodproofed or elevated to or above the Regulatory Flood Elevation; 3.7.2.3 Reasonable repair of structures constructed with the written authorization required by A.R.S. s 48-3613; 3.7.2.4 Facilities constructed or installed pursuant to a Certificate of Environmental Compatibility issued pursuant to A.R.S. Title 40, Chapter 2, Article 6.2; and 3.7.2.5 In accordance with A.R.S. s 48-3613(D), in addition to other penalties or remedies otherwise provided by law, this state, a political subdivision or a person who may be damaged or has been damaged as a result of the unauthorized diversion, retardation or obstruction of a watercourse has the right to commence, maintain and prosecute any appropriate action or pursue any remedy to enjoin, abate or otherwise prevent any person from violating or continuing to violate this section or regulations adopted pursuant to A.R.S. Title 48, Chapter 21, Article 1. If a person is found to be in violation of this Section, the court shall require the violator to either comply with this Section if authorized by the Floodplain Board or remove the Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

3.8 UNLAWFUL ACTS 3.8.1 It is unlawful for a person to engage in any development or to divert, retard or obstruct the flow of waters in a watercourse if it creates a hazard to life or property without securing the written authorization required by A.R.S. s 48-3613. Where the watercourse is a delineated floodplain, it is unlawful to engage in any development affecting the flow of waters without securing written authorization required by A.R.S. $ 48-3613. 3.8.2 Any person found guilty of violating any provision of this ordinance shall be guilty Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance. Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

3.9 DECLARATION OF PUBLIC NUISANCE STATUTE All development located or maintained within any Special Flood Hazard Area after August 8, 1973, in violation of this ordinance, is a public nuisance per se and may be abated, prevented or restrained by action of this political subdivision. 3.10 ABATEMENT OF VIOLATIONS Within 60 days of discovery of a violation of this ordinance, the Floodplain Administrator shall submit a report to the Floodplain Board which shall include all information available to the Floodplain Administrator which is pertinent to said violation. Within 60 days of receipt of this report, the Floodplain Board shall either: 3.10.1 Take any necessary action to effect the abatement of such violation; or 3.10.2 Issue a variance to this ordinance in accordance with the provisions of Section 6.0 herein; or. 3.10.3 Order the owner of the property upon which the violation exists to provide. whatever additional information may be required for their determination. Such information must be provided to the Floodplain Administrator within 60 days of such order and the Floodplain Administrator shall submit an amended report to the Floodplain Board within 60 days. At the next regularly scheduled public meeting, the Floodplain Board shall either order the abatement of said violation or they shall grant a variance in accordance with the provisions of Section 6.0 herein; or. 3.10.4 Submit to the Federal Emergency Management Agency a declaration for denial of. insurance, stating that the property is in violation of a cited state or local law, regulation or ordinance, pursuant to Section 1316 of the National Flood Insurance Act of 1968 as amended.. 3.11 SEVERABILITY This ordinance and the various parts thereof are hereby declared to be severable. Should any Section of this ordinance be declared by the courts to be unconstitutional or invalid, such decision shall not affect the validity of the ordinance as a whole, or any portion thereof other than the Section so declared to be unconstitutional or invalid. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

SECTION 4.0 ADMINISTRATION 4.1 DESIGNATION OF THE FLOODPLAIN ADMINISTRATOR The County Engineer, or other person named by Floodplain Board, is hereby appointed to administer, implement and enforce this ordinance by granting or denying development permits in accordance with its provisions..

section: 4.2: DUTIES AND RESPONSIBILITIES OF THE FLOODPLAIN ADMINISTRATOR
text: 4.2 DUTIES AND RESPONSIBILITIES OF THE FLOODPLAIN ADMINISTRATOR Duties of the Floodplain Administrator shall include, but not be limited to: 4.2.1 PERMIT REVIEW - Review all development permits to determine that: 4.2.1.1 The permit requirements of this ordinance have been satisfied; 4.2.1.2 All other required state and federal permits have been obtained; 4.2.4.3 The site is reasonably safe from flooding;. 4.2.4.4 In areas where a floodway has not been designated, the proposed development does not adversely affect the carrying capacity of areas where Base Flood Elevations have been determined.. 4.2.2 SUBSTANTIAL IMPROVEMENT AND SUBSTANTIAL DAMAGE ASSESSMENTS Review all development permits for improvements and/or damages to existing structures to determine if the application of the substantial improvement rules apply, including establishing a definition of market value determination and verifying that the estimated improvement and/or repair costs are less than 50% of the market value of the structure.. 4.2.3 USE OF OTHER BASE FLOOD DATA When Base Flood Elevation data has not been provided in accordance with Section 3.2. the Floodplain Administrator shall obtain, review and reasonably utilize any Base Flood Elevation data available from a federal, state or other source, in order to administer Section 5.0 The Floodplain administrator may require the Base Flood Elevation to be determine by an. applicant before issuing a Development permit. Any such information shall be consistent with the requirements of the Federal Emergency Management Agency and the Director of the Arizona Department of Water Resources and may be submitted to the Floodplain Board for adoption. 4.2.4 OBTAIN AND MAINTAIN FOR PUBLIC INSPECTION Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance. 4.2.5 NOTIFICATION TO OTHER ENTITIES 4.2.5.1 Whenever a watercourse is to be altered or relocated: 4.2.5.1.1 Notify adjacent communities and the Arizona Department of Water Resources prior to such alteration or relocation of a watercourse, and submit evidence of such notification to the Federal Emergency Management Agency through appropriate notification means; and 4.2.5.1.2 Assure that the flood carrying capacity of the altered or relocated portion of said watercourse be maintained. 4.2.5.2 Base Flood Elevation and rate of flow due to physical alterations: 4.2.5.2.1 Base Flood Elevations may increase or decrease resulting from physical changes affecting flooding conditions. As soon as practicable, but not later than six months after the date such information becomes available, the Floodplain Administrator shall notify the Federal Emergency Management Agency of the changes by submitting technical or scientific data in accordance with Volume 44 Code of Federal Regulations Section 65.3. Such a submission is necessary so that upon confirmation of those physical changes affecting flooding conditions, risk premium rates and floodplain management requirements will be based upon current data. 4.2.5.2.2 Within one hundred twenty (120) days after completion of Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

4.2.6 MAP DETERMINATIONS Make interpretations, where needed, as to the exact location of the boundaries of the special flood hazard areas (e.g., where there appears to be a conflict between a mapped boundary and actual field conditions). The person contesting the location of the boundary shall be given a reasonable opportunity to appeal the interpretation as provided in Section 6.0. 4.2.7 REMEDIAL ACTIONS Take actions on violations of this ordinance as required in Section 3.10 herein

4.3 ESTABLISHMENT OF DEVELOPMENT PERMIT A Development Permit shall be obtained before construction or development begins, including placement of manufactured homes, within any Special Flood Hazard Area established in Section 3.2. Application for a Development Permit shall be made on forms furnished by the Floodplain Administrator and may include, but not be limited to, plans in duplicate drawn to scale showing the nature, location, dimensions and elevation of the area in question, existing or proposed structures, fill, storage of materials, drainage facilities and the location of the foregoing. Specifically, the following information is required: 4.3.1 Proposed elevation in relation to mean sea level of the lowest floor (including basement) of all structures. In Zone AO, elevation of existing highest adjacent grade and proposed elevation of lowest floor of all structures; 4.3.2 Proposed elevation in relation to mean sea level to which any non-residential structure will be floodproofed; 4.3.3 Certification by a registered professional engineer or architect that the floodproofing methods for any nonresidential structure meet the floodproofing criteria in Section 5.1.3.2; 4.3.4 Base Flood Elevation data for subdivision proposals or other development greater than 50 lots or 5 acres, whichever is the lesser; and Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance. SECTION 5.0

5.1 STANDARDS OF CONSTRUCTION In all special flood hazard areas the following standards are required:. 5.1.1 Anchoring 5.1.1.1 All new construction and substantial improvements shall be anchored to prevent flotation, collapse or lateral movement of the structure resulting from hydrodynamic and hydrostatic loads, including the effects of buoyancy; and. 5.1.1.2 All manufactured homes shall meet the anchoring standards of Section 5.5.2. 5.1.2 Construction Materials and Methods 5.1.2.1 All new construction and substantial improvements shall be constructed with materials and utility equipment resistant to flood damage;. 5.1.2.2 All new construction and substantial improvements shall be constructed using methods and practices that minimize flood damage; and. 5.1.2.3 Within Zones AH or AO, adequate drainage paths shall be constructed around structures on slopes to guide floodwaters around and away from proposed structures.. 5.1.3 Elevation and Floodproofing 5.1.3.1 Residential Construction Residential construction, new or substantial improvement, shall have the lowest floor, including basement, elevated to or above the Regulatory Flood Elevation. 5.1.3.1.1 In a Zone AO, the Base Flood Elevation is determined from the. FIRM panel. If unspecified, the required elevation is at minimum two (2) feet above highest. adjacent grade. 5.1.3.1.2 In a Zone A where the Base Flood Elevation has not been. determined, the Base Flood Elevation is determined locally by the criteria set out in Section 4.2.3 or may be established at least 1 feet above the highest adjacent grade by the Floodplain Administrator in accordance with Section 4.2.6. As defined, the "Regulatory Flood Elevation" is one foot above the "Base Flood Elevation.". 5.1.3.1.3 In Zones AE and AH, the Base Flood Elevation is determined. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

Detached garages, storage structures and other accessory structures not meeting the above standards must be constructed in accordance with all applicable standards as appropriate . Upon completion of the structure, appropriate certification that the requirements of this. section have been satisfied shall be provided to the Floodplain Administrator for verification 5.1.3.5 Flood Openings All new construction and substantial improvement with fully enclosed areas below the lowest floor (excluding basements) that are usable solely for parking of vehicles, building access or storage, and which are subject to flooding, shall be designed to automatically equalize hydrostatic flood forces on exterior walls by allowing for the automatic entry and exit of floodwater. Designs for meeting this requirement must meet or exceed the following criteria: For non-engineered openings: 5.1.3.5.1 Have a minimum of two openings, on different sides of each enclosed area, having a total net area of not less than one square inch for every square foot of enclosed area subject to flooding; and, 5.1.3.5.2 The bottom of all openings shall be no higher than one foot above grade;. and, 5.1.3.5.3 Openings may be equipped with screens, louvers, valves, or other. coverings or devices provided that they permit the automatic entry and exit of floodwater; or 5.1.3.5.4 Alternatively, a registered engineer or architect may design and certify. engineered openings. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

5.2 STANDARDS FOR STORAGE OF MATERIALS AND EQUIPMENT 5.2.1 The storage or processing of materials that could be injurious to human, animal or plant life if released due to damage from flooding is prohibited in special flood hazard areas. 5.2.2 Storage of other material or equipment may be allowed if not subject to damage by floods and if firmly anchored to prevent flotation, or if readily removable from the area within the time available after flood warning 5.3 STANDARDS FOR WATER SUPPLY AND WASTE DISPOSAL SYSTEMS 5.3.1 All new or replacement water supply and sanitary sewage systems shall be. designed to minimize or eliminate infiltration of flood waters into the system and discharge from systems into flood waters. 5.3.2 On-site waste disposal systems shall be located to avoid impairment to them or contamination from them during flooding. 5.3.3 Waste disposal systems shall not be installed wholly or partially in a regulatory floodway.

5.4 ADDITIONAL DEVELOPMENT STANDARDS, INCLUDING SUBDIVISIONS 5.4.1 All new subdivision proposals and other proposed development (including proposals for manufactured home parks and subdivisions), greater than 50 lots or 5 acres, whichever is the lesser, shall: 5.4.1.1 Identify the special flood hazard area and the elevation of the base flood; and 5.4.1.2 Identify on the final plans the elevation(s) of the proposed structure(s) and pads. If the site is filled above the Base Flood Elevation, the final lowest floor and grade. elevations shall be certified by a registered professional engineer or surveyor and provided to the Floodplain Administrator. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

5.5 STANDARDS FOR MANUFACTURED HOMES All manufactured homes that are placed on site or substantially improved shall 5.5.1 Be elevated to conform with Section 5.1.3.1 as appropriate so that the bottom of the structural frame or the lowest point of any attached appliances, whichever is lower, is to or above the Regulatory Flood Elevation; and 5.5.2 Be securely anchored to an adequately anchored foundation system to resist flotation, collapse or lateral movement. Methods of anchoring may include, but are not to be. limited to, use of over-the-top or frame ties to ground anchors. This requirement is in addition to applicable state and local anchoring requirements for resisting wind forces. 5.5.3 Upon completion of installation of the manufactured home, appropriate certification that the elevation requirements of this section have been satisfied shall be provided to the. Floodplain Administrator for verification..

5.6 STANDARDS FOR RECREATIONAL VEHICLES All recreational vehicles placed on site shall:. 5.6.1 Be on site for fewer than 180 consecutive days, 5.6.2 Be fully licensed and ready for highway use. A recreational vehicle is ready for highway use if it is on its wheels or jacking system, is attached to the site only by quick disconnect type utilities and security devices, and has no permanently attached additions; or 5.6.3 Meet the permit requirements of Section 4.0 of this ordinance and the elevation and. anchoring requirements for manufactured homes in Section 5.5. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance. Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

5.7 FLOODWAYS Located within the special flood hazard areas established in Section 3.2 are areas designated as floodways. Since the floodway is an extremely hazardous area due to the velocity of floodwaters which carry debris, potential projectiles and erosion potential, the following provisions apply: 5.7.1 Prohibit encroachments, including fill, new construction, substantial improvements. and other development, unless certification by a registered professional engineer or architect is provided demonstrating that encroachments shall not result in any increase in flood levels during the occurrence of the base flood discharge. 5.7.2 If Section 5.7 is satisfied, all new construction and substantial improvements shall. comply with all other applicable flood hazard reduction provisions of Section 5.0 .. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

SECTION 6.0

6.1 NATURE OF VARIANCES The variance criteria set forth in this section of the ordinance are based on the general principle of zoning law that variances pertain to a piece of property and are not personal in nature. A variance may be granted for a parcel of property with physical characteristics so unusual that complying with the requirements of this ordinance would create an exceptional hardship to the applicant or the surrounding property owners. The characteristics must be unique to the property and not be shared by adjacent parcels. The unique characteristic must pertain to the land itself, not to the structure, its inhabitants or the property owners. It is the duty of the Flood Control District of Greenlee County to help protect its citizens from flooding. This need is so compelling and the implications of the cost of insuring a structure built below the Regulatory Flood Elevation are so serious that variances from the flood elevation or from other requirements in the flood ordinance are quite rare. The long-term goal of preventing and reducing flood loss and damage can only be met if variances are strictly limited. Therefore, the variance guidelines provided in this ordinance are more detailed and contain multiple provisions that must be met before a variance can be properly granted. The criteria are designed to screen out those situations in which alternatives other than a variance are more appropriate.

6.2 FLOODPLAIN REVIEW BOARD 6.2.1 The Board of Supervisors may establish a board of review, which may be the. advisory board or a committee of the advisory board to sit in review and make decisions. 6.2.2 The Floodplain Review Board shall hear and decide appeals when it is alleged there is an error in any requirement, decision, or determination made by the Floodplain Administrator in the enforcement or administration of this ordinance. 6.2.3 In considering such applications, the Floodplain Review Board shall consider all. technical evaluations, all relevant factors, standards specified in other sections of this ordinance, and: 6.2.3.1 The danger that materials may be swept onto other lands to the injury of others; 6.2.3.2 The danger of life and property due to flooding or erosion damage; 6.2.3.3 The susceptibility of the proposed facility and its contents to flood damage. and the effect of such damage on the individual owner; 6.2.3.4 The importance of the services provided by the proposed facility to the community; Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

6.2.3.5 The necessity to the facility of a waterfront location, where applicable:.
6.2.3.6 The availability of alternative locations for the proposed use, which are not
subject to flooding or erosion damage;
6.2.3.7 The compatibility of the proposed use with existing and anticipated
development;
6.2.3.8 The relationship of the proposed use to the comprehensive plan and.
floodplain management program for that area;
6.2.3.9 The safety of access to the property in time of flood for ordinary and.
emergency vehicles;
6.2.3.10 The expected heights, velocity, duration, rate of rise, and sediment.
transport of the flood waters expected at the site; and,
6.2.3.11 The costs of providing governmental services during and after flood.
conditions, including maintenance and repair of public utilities and facilities such as sewer, gas.
electrical, water system and streets and bridges.
6.2.4 Upon consideration of the factors of Section 6.2.3 and the purposes of this
ordinance, the Floodplain Review Board may attach such conditions to the granting of variances
as it deems necessary to further the purposes of this ordinance.
6.2.5 Any applicant to whom a variance is granted shall be given written notice over the

6.2.5.2 Such construction below the regulatory flood elevation increases risks to
life and property; and.

The land upon which the variance is granted shall be ineligible for

Recorder and shall be recorded in a manner so that it appears in the chain of title of the affected

6.2.6 The Floodplain Administrator shall maintain a record of all variance actions.
including justification for their issuance.

6.3 CONDITIONS FOR VARIANCES 6.3.1 Variances shall only be issued: 6.3.1.1 Upon a determination that the granting of a variance will not result in increased flood heights, additional threats to public safety, extraordinary public expense, create. nuisances, cause fraud on or victimization of the public or conflict with existing local laws or ordinances; 6.3.1 2 For the repair, rehabilitation or restoration of structures listed in the National Register of Historic Places or the State Inventory of Historic Places, upon a determination that the proposed repair or rehabilitation will not preclude the structures' continued designation as a historic structure and the variance is the minimum necessary to preserve the. historic character and design of the structure; 6.3.1.3 Upon a determination that the variance is the minimum necessary,. considering the flood hazard, to afford relief, 6.3.1.4 Upon a showing of good and sufficient cause;. 6.3.1.5 Upon a determination that failure to grant the variance would result in exceptional hardship to the applicant; 6.3.1.6 Upon a showing that the use cannot perform its intended purpose unless it. is located or carried out in close proximity to water. This includes only facilities defined in. Section 2.0 of this ordinance in the definition of "Functionally Dependent Use." 6.3.2 Variances shall not be issued within any floodway if any increase in flood levels during the base flood discharge would result. 6.3.3 Generally, variances may be issued for new construction and substantial improvements to be erected on a lot of one-half acre or less in size contiguous to and surrounded by lots with existing structures constructed below the Regulatory Flood Elevation, provided the procedures of Sections 4.0 and 5.0 of this ordinance have been fully considered. As the lot size increases beyond one-half acre, the technical justification required for issuing the variance. increases. Greenlee County Flood Control Ordinance Revision 1 May 3, 2016 06/29/2016

APPENDIX A Accessory use. A use which is incidental and subordinate to the principal use of the parcel of land on which it is located. Alluvial fan: A geomorphologic feature characterized by a cone or fan shaped deposit of boulders, gravel, and fine sediments that have been eroded from mountain slopes, transported by flood flows, and then deposited on the valley floors, and which is subject to flash flooding, high velocity flows, debris flows, erosion, sediment movement and deposition, and channel migration Apex: A point on an alluvial fan or similar landform below which the flow path of the major. stream that formed the fan becomes unpredictable and alluvial fan flooding can occur.. Area of special flood-related erosion hazard: The land within a community which is most likely to be subject to severe flood-related erosion losses. Determining Market Value of Existing Structures: "Market value" shall be determined by estimating the cost to replace the structure in new condition and adjusting that cost figure by the amount of depreciation which has accrued since the structure was constructed..
1) The cost of replacement of the structure shall be based on a square foot cost factor
determined by reference to a building cost estimating guide recognized by the building construction industry; and,
2) The amount of depreciation shall be determined by taking into account the age and
physical deterioration of the structure and functional obsolescence as approved by the floodplain administrator, but shall not include economic or other forms of external obsolescence; and,
3) Use of replacement costs or accrued depreciation factors different from those contained.
in recognized building cost estimating guides may be considered only if such factors are included in a report prepared by an independent professional appraiser and supported by a written explanation of the differences; or May be 125% of the Full Cash Valve as determined by the County Assessor; or,. May be determined by the Owner by an appraisal of the property prior to damage... Existing manufactured home park or subdivision. A manufactured home park or subdivision for which the construction of facilities for servicing the lots on which the manufactured homes are to. be affixed (including, at a minimum, the installation of utilities, the construction of streets, and either final site grading or the pouring of concrete pads) is completed before {insert date your first floodplain management ordinance was adopted}

Page 2 of 6

June 29, 2016 Expansion to an existing manufactured home park or subdivision. The preparation of additional sites by the construction of facilities for servicing the lots on which the manufactured homes are. to be affixed (including the installation of utilities, the construction of streets, and either final site grading or the pouring of concrete pads).. Flood-related erosion: The collapse or subsidence of land along the shore of a lake or other body of water as a result of undermining caused by waves or currents of water exceeding anticipated cyclical level or suddenly caused by an unusually high water level in a natural body of water. accompanied by a severe storm, or by an unanticipated force of nature, such as a flash flood or an abnormal tidal surge, or by some similarly unusual and unforeseeable event which results in flooding. Flood-related erosion area or Flood-related erosion prone area A land area adjoining the shore of a lake or other body of water, which due to the composition of the shoreline or bank and high water levels or wind-driven currents, is likely to suffer flood-related erosion damage. Flood-related erosion area management: The operation of an overall program of corrective and preventive measures for reducing flood-related erosion damage, including but not limited to emergency preparedness plans, flood-related erosion control works, and floodplain management regulations. Floodway fringe: That area of the floodplain on either side of the "Regulatory Floodway" where encroachment may be permitted. Fraud and victimization: As related to Section 6.0 of this ordinance, means that the variance granted must not cause fraud on or victimization of the public. In examining this requirement, the {community governing body} will consider the fact that every newly constructed building adds to government responsibilities and remains a part of the community for fifty to one hundred years. Buildings that are permitted to be constructed below the Regulatory Flood Elevation are subject during all those years to increased risk of damage from floods, while future owners of the property and the community as a whole are subject to all the costs, inconvenience, danger, and suffering that those increased flood damages bring. In addition, future owners may purchase the property, unaware that it is subject to potential flood damage, and can be insured only at very high flood insurance rates. Levee: A man made structure, usually an earthen embankment, designed and constructed in accordance with sound engineering practices to contain, control or divert the flow of water so as to provide protection from temporary flooding.. Levee system: A flood protection system which consists of a levee, or levees, and associated structures, such as closure and drainage devices, which are constructed and operated in accordance with sound engineering practices. New manufactured home park or subdivision: A manufactured home park or subdivision for

Page 3 of 6

June 29, 2016 which the construction of facilities for servicing the lots on which the manufactured homes are to be affixed (including at a minimum, the installation of utilities, the construction of streets, and either final site grading or the pouring of concrete pads) is completed on or after the effective date of floodplain management regulations adopted by the community. Program deficiency: A defect in a community's floodplain management regulations or administrative procedures that impairs effective implementation of those floodplain management regulations. Public safety and nuisance: As related to Section 6 of this ordinance, means that the granting of a variance must not result in anything which is injurious to the safety or health of an entire community or neighborhood, or any considerable number of persons, or unlawfully obstructs the free passage or use, in the customary manner, of any navigable lake, or river, bay, stream, canal, or basin. Remedy a violation: To bring the structure or other development into compliance with Federal. State or local floodplain management regulations, or if this is not possible, to reduce the impacts of its noncompliance. Ways that impacts may be reduced include protecting the structure or other affected development from flood damages, implementing the enforcement provisions of the ordinance or otherwise deterring future similar violations, or reducing State or Federal financial exposure with regard to the structure or other development.

section: Cochise County LEPC Hazardous Materials Response Plan
text:
Letter of Promulgation. This Plan complies with the following: Emergency Planning and Community Right to Know Act (EPcRA) was passed in 1986 as an amendment to the Superfund Amendments and Reauthorization Act (SARA) III and. Arizona Revised Statutes 49-125 Comprehensive Emergency Response Plans. This is a stand-alone plan yet is included as Annex 1 in the Cochise County Emergency Opeations Plan adopted in
2024. This Plan is the property of the Cochise County Emergency Management (CCEM)..
The Plan does not contain specific facility information or chemical inventories due to security concerns. Protected chemical information data is available electronically to public safety agencies as outlined within the Plan. The public must request chemical inventory information through the procedural requirements set up by the Arizona Emergency Response Commission (AZSERC). This Plan does not contain tactical-level hazardous materials response procedures as those are maintained by the Fire District/Departments in Cochise County with response capabilities and duties.. This Plan supersedes all prior Cochise County Hazardous Materials Incident Response andor Recovery Plans, including those in the current revision of the CCEOP. Users of this Plan are encouraged to advise the CcEM of areas where the Plan can be corrected and improved. This Plan is considered a living document in that the contents are continually reviewed. The Plan will be updated as conditions, techniques, and understanding progress and be revised annually at a minimum. Approved by the Cochise County Local Emergency Planning Committee:. See attached LEPC minutes Local Emergency Planning Committee Date Daniel S. Duchon 8/21/2025 Cochise County Emergency Management Date

Approval & Implementation

The Cochise County Local Emergency Planning Committee (CCLEPC) will review and update this Plan annually as required by listed authorities. AZDEQ reviews the Plan for compliance, then the CCLEPC Membership reviews and accepts the plan revisions at a public meeting and officially adopts the Plan. The CCEM will maintain the master copy to: Incorporate necessary changes; Maintain a listing of designated owner copies; and Provide printed copies upon proper request. Changes to this Plan will be submitted to the Cochise County Emergency Manager for review or revisions and necessary approvals. This Plan may be activated in parts or its entirety. The activated elements of this Plan should be based on assessing the released hazardous material, the situation found, resource availability, and the safety of the public and first responders. ii

section_path: Cochise County LEPC Hazardous Materials Response Plan

text: Purpose, Scope, Situation Overview, and Planning Assumptions 1.1Purpose The purpose of this Plan is to provide emergency responders, affected facilities, local governments, and the public with background data, organizational responsibilities, and operating procedures so that they can better plan for, effectively respond to, and expeditiously recover from spills, releases, or other incidents involving hazardous materials. There are various definitions, including hazardous materials, hazardous substances, hazardous waste, or hazardous chemicals, under several federal and state authorities, including the United States (us) Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the US Department of Transportation (DOt), the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OsHA), and Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (AzDEQ). For this document, "hazardous materials" is a general term covering all of these unless specifically stated or referenced otherwise. Hazardous materials are ubiquitous in our society. We have them in our homes, workplaces, stores, medical facilities, and vehicles. They are transported over the road, in the air, on the water, and in pipelines. Hazards materials are necessary in our modern world. Various state and federal agencies have jurisdiction over hazardous materials within the scope of the law. These various laws cover hazardous materials within the workplace, how they are transported and stored, and how they are used and disposed of. This Plan serves as an Annex 9 in the Cochise County Emergency Operations Plan (CCeOP). 1.2Scope This Plan has been developed to comply with the Superfund Amendments and Reauthorization Act (SARA) of 1986) Title Ill, which specifies that each Local Emergency Planning Committee (LEPc) prepares an emergency plan that guides response to an incident involving hazardous materials in general and specifically for extremely hazardous substances (EHs), as defined by the EPA, within the unincorporated areas of the County; and to guide local municipalities in developing or revising their plans. Many tasks, procedures, and provisions in other Cochise County plans, such as the CCEOP, equally apply to hazardous materials response and are not repeated in this Plan. This Plan was originally developed using the ADEQ Hazardous Materials Emergency Planning (HMep) Grant. Conditions of this grant and EPcRA place responsibility for the development, security, and maintenance of this Plan on the Local Emergency Planning Committee in each jurisdiction in each state. The CCLEPC and the CCEM actively work with the AZDEQ to maintain good standing for HMEP grant requirements to maintain this Plan and support local response activities. The CceOp is the umbrella plan for protecting health, safety, property, and the environment from non-military incidents in the County's unincorporated areas. It serves as a guide for emergency planning in other jurisdictions for Iocal emergencies. It identifies mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery procedures relevant to all hazards. Other jurisdictions in the County have Emergency Operations Plans or Standard Operational Procedures (sOPs) or Standard Operational Guidelines (SOGs) for response to hazardous material incidents. These plans and guides should be revised to reflect information and general planning, response, and recovery tenets contained in this Plan. Cochise County participates in the Southern Region Hazmat Group with Cochise, Santa Cruz, Pima, and Yuma counties. This group works on standardizing equipment, SOGs, and other hazardous materials response issues. Each fixed facility with hazardous substances at or above defined threshold levels must develop an on-site contingency plan that specifies notification procedures, emergency response organization and responsibilities, emergency response organization procedures, and coordination procedures for interfacing with off-site authorities and response organizations. Facilities not required to do so by law are encouraged to develop such a plan and provide a copy to the CCLEPC and the AZsERC to be added to the Tier II database. Response organizations are important partners in the implementation of this Plan. Attachment B contains a list of response agencies within Cochise County..

Hazmat Group with Cochise, Santa Cruz, Pima, and Yuma counties. This group works on standardizing equipment, SOGs, and other hazardous materials response issues. Each fixed facility with hazardous substances at or above defined threshold levels must develop an on-site contingency plan that specifies notification procedures, emergency response organization and responsibilities, emergency response organization procedures, and coordination procedures for interfacing with off-site authorities and response organizations. Facilities not required to do so by law are encouraged to develop such a plan and provide a copy to the CCLEPC and the AZsERC to be added to the Tier II database. Response organizations are important partners in the implementation of this Plan. Attachment B contains a list of response agencies within Cochise County..

This Plan may be implemented upon notification to the CceM Director that a hazardous material incident occurred or for planning, training, exercise, after-action, or recovery activities. Local response agency heads may activate the Plan as well. This Plan will be activated when EHS materials are involved, evacuation or shelter orders are required, more than one jurisdictional agency is required for response, or at the request of any federal, state, or local agencies. Situation Overview. Environmental regulations began in the 1950s to protect our air, water, soil, and persons from exposure to hazardous materials. These include the Federal Water Pollution Control Act (FwpcA), the Clean Air Act (cAA), the Clean Water Act (CWA), CERCLA, SARA, and EPCRA. On October 17th, 1986, the United States Congress enacted EPCRA to ensure that the citizens in each community in the United States are safe from the harmful effects of hazardous material. releases. EPcRA was not only created to ensure the safety of citizens; this law requires the formation of planning groups, the existence and maintenance of community plans, reporting requirements for businesses having hazardous materials types and amounts, and the responsibilities of planning groups, first responders, and community leaders. EPCRA and the AzseRC require this Plan according to the authorities listed. This Plan contains criteria for the various actions to occur during a hazardous material release. This Plan is intended to provide an overview of the expected. actions by county citizens, businesses, organizations, responders, or senior community leaders in the event of a hazardous materials release in Cochise County. The information in this Plan is to provide the latest and the most up- to-date information related to the actions expected to protect life safety, stabilize the incident, and preserve. property and the environment in case of a hazardous materials release. 2.1Hazard Analysis Summary The locations in Cochise County with the highest potential for a major hazardous materials incident include (not necessarily in order of probability): Highways, roads, streets, and bulk transfer and service points; Railways and railway sidings;. Chemical manufacturing plants; Electronics assembly facilities;. Fuel depots, including liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) and petroleum distribution facilities;. Pesticide and fertilizer storage and distribution centers; Water treatment facilities that use aqueous chlorine-containing solutions or gaseous chlorine, including public water sources, sewage treatment plants, and some public swimming pools; andt. Mining operations that produce or otherwise use significant quantities of acids, cyanide, ammonium nitrate products, or other extremely hazardous substances. Major transportation routes in Cochise County that carry hazardous materials are shown in Attachment A. Hazardous materials may be transported on any road in the county. These major transportation routes act as evacuation routes in coordination with the CcEM, Cochise County Sheriff's Department, Cochise County Public Works, the Arizona Department of Transportation (AzDoT), and the Arizona Department of Public Safety (AzDps). Facilities and transportation routes of facilities that store, receive, or transport extremely hazardous substances (EHs) are maintained in the AZsERc Tier II database and with the CCEM and the fire departments/districts having jurisdiction.. In 2022, AzDEQ funded a commodity flow study for southern Arizona. Three sites were surveyed in Cochise County, including the intersections of State Route (SR) 80/US 191 in Douglas, SR 191 and I 10 in Willcox, and SR 186 and I 10 in Willcox. The intersection of SR82 and SR83 is not in Cochise County, but SR82 continues into Cochise County east. of the intersection, so it is included. Still, there is no breakdown of the direction of travel in the surveys. The results include placarded loads of DOT-regulated hazardous materials through those locations over three 12-hour surveys.

Cochise County LEPC Hazardous Materials Response Plan. in dangerous conditions requiring immediate corrective action by facility or emergency personnel to protect responders, the public, property, and the environment.. The following additional planning assumptions apply to this Plan:
1. There are fixed facilities containing hazardous materials throughout Cochise County in varying amounts.
Facilities may not be required to report, or they may not report, their storage or use of hazardous materials.
2. Hazardous materials move throughout the county through multiple modes of transportation (see
Attachment A): a. A major interstate highway, Interstate 10, crosses through the County, and hazardous materials are transported along this route daily. Hazardous materials also move along state, county, and. Iocal feeder roads to or from their final destinations.. b. Cochise County has several rail lines, ports of entry, and airports where hazardous materials may move in commerce.. C. Not all hazardous materials in transportation will be marked or identified easily. The DOT regulations allow certain classes of hazardous materials under certain amounts to be excepted. from placarding..
3. The availability of first responders in certain areas of the County is limited due to limited coverage areas by
Fire Districts/Departments. This may delay response to hazardous materials incidents.. Individual Fire Departments/Districts may not have the specialized training or equipment to respond to a hazardous materials incident requiring activation of local mutual aid or reliance on state or federal resources.
5. Hazardous materials incidents can be unpredictable depending on location, container, damage, release
rate, weather conditions, etc.
6. Initial reports of hazardous materials incidents are often incomplete, making ongoing situational
assessments a priority..
7. Residents may evacuate spontaneously without official order or recommendations in a serious or
widespread hazardous materials incident..
8. Cochise County, the State of Arizona, the Federal Government, or private sector agencies may provide
resources to augment local forces in hazardous materials releases.. Hazardous materials incidents may require long-term recovery and rehabilitation efforts beyond the scope of first responders and this Plan.. Concept of Operations 3.1 Concept of Operations. Local and county governments and facility owners primarily protect public health and safety during hazardous materials incidents. Local firefighters, emergency medical providers, law enforcement officers, or other first responders are usually first on the scene of hazardous materials incidents. This Plan can be implemented by listed agencies or facility emergency coordinators in full or in part as required.. The local fire department/district or first responder agency assumes command initially. Notifications are made to jurisdictions having authority as a priority. Unified command (uc) is encouraged where multiple jurisdictions have primacy, especially for significant releases. Facility response coordinators can participate in UC releases from facilities requiring notification by the facility owner/responsible party (Rp) according to the statutes they fall under. Hazardous substances released in reportable quantities (RQs) are to be reported by the RP Depending on the event's severity, the Fire Department/Districts should notify the Cochise County Emergency Management (CCEM) or AzsERC of releases, including a follow-up report. These reports are important for incident tracking and grant award documentation. The RP must follow up on releases as they are legally required to. Any emergency declarations are handled per the CCEOP with the coordination of the CCEM, which provides incident support for on-scene activities at hazardous materials incidents. CCEM maintains this Plan, along with the CCEOP, and assists the CCLEPC with associated activities.

The Sister City Plan provides information on the response of US personnel to Agua Prieta and Naco, Sonora, Mexico.
Releases within 100 km or 62 miles of the border or response of US personnel to Mexico require notification to the
National Response Center (NRC) and additional notifications to Mexican authorities.
Hazardous materials teams are designated at the local fire department level and comply with the National Fire
Protection Association (NFPA), OSHA, and state requirements. SARA and OSHA regulations (CFR 1910.120) require
that any worker who may be exposed to hazardous materials during response to an incident must be properly
trained. AzDEMA/EM manages training programs that meet or exceed national standards for personnel responding
to hazardous materials incidents. Incorporated cities/towns, fire districts, private sector facilities, and other
organizations identify the level of training that best serves their purposes and requirements. Training for awareness,
operations, and technician-level responders is provided through AZDEMA/EM and coordinated through CCEM. Al
fire department members should be trained to operations level and capable of defensive actions during hazardous
materials incidents. All first responders should be trained to the awareness level and capable of initial identification,
size up, and implementing protective actions. Technician-level responders are trained and equipped to implement
higher complexity offensive actions during hazardous materials incidents.
Cochise County has three fire department/district-level hazardous materials teams with equipment caches for
response. The equipment caches include items listed in Attachment C. Cochise County participates in the Southern
Region Hazardous Materials Group to coordinate procedures, equipment, funding, training, and other opportunities.
This group helps acquire the State Homeland Security Grant Program (SHsGP) and other grants for equipment and
training.
The decision to evacuate or shelter in place is made according to incident specifics and the determination by
hazardous materials technicians or, if applicable, the facility response coordinator (FRc). Evacuations will be
coordinated through incident command/unified command (ic/uc), the ccEM, and other departments and
jurisdictions. Hazardous materials incidents will follow the National Incident Management System's (Nims's)
Incident Command System (ICS) methodology.
Hazmat incident identification involves the rapid identification, detection, and confirmation of the incident. Incident
identification also defines notification and action triggers. During the response and recovery periods, Ic/uc and
trained hazardous materials responders will evaluate and analyze information regarding identifying, securing,
removing, and disposing of hazardous materials. When an event is focused in scope to a specific type or response
mode, technical and subject matter expertise may be provided by personnel from a supporting agency with skills
pertinent to the type of event, who will advise or direct operations within the context of the ICS structure.
The following general practices should take place during a suspected hazardous materials incident by responders:
Assume command and institute a command structure;
Identify the material(s) and initiate protective action distances and secure the scene from unauthorized
entry;
Institute notifications and request additional resources based on location and need;
Contact the Community Coordinator through cCEM and, when appropriate, the FRC to respond to the
incident command post (IcP) to provide expertise, support, and coordination;
Utilize defensive actions according to department SOPs/SOGs, such as diking ahead of the spill, covering the
material, or utilizing fog streams;.
Initiate evacuation or shelter in place if necessary. Technicians will use software such as CAMEO, MARPLOT,
and ALOHA provided by the EPA's Office of Response and Restoration or other proprietary software to
determine the area affected, vulnerabilities, and populations. Routes from affected sites or areas should be
based on the type of release, the chemicals involved (utilizing pre-plans, information on site, or Tier Il), the
amount and rate of release, and weather conditions;
O HAzMAT Technician availability: Fry Fire District, Sunsites-Pearce Fire District, Douglas Fire
Department, ADEQ - Emergency Response Unit, ADOT HAZMAT Team, AZDPS HAZMAT Team.
Initiate public notifications through the CCEM;
Identify and notify the RP, if possible, to provide cleanup and disposal resources;
Establish communications among response agencies using the Cochise County Tactical Communications
Plan (TICP);

text:
4.7 Cochise County Emergency Management (CcEM) The Director of the CCEM is the designated Community Coordinator per EPRCA 303. Additionally, CCEM: Coordinates hazardous materials training for the County and CCLEPC; Maintains updated listings of hazardous materials facilities in conjunction with the CCLEPC;. Provides the public, facilities, local jurisdictions, fire departments/districts, and other departments and agencies with hazardous material data and updates of this Plan; Provides administrative support to the CCLEPC; Identifies community lifelines and underserved community vulnerabilities to hazardous materials releases; Identifies underserved communities or vulnerable populations who reside in the vulnerability zone around the facility who will need special consideration in the event of an emergency requiring shelter in place or evacuations; Reference EOP Annex 9, Part 7: Critical Facility List for list of facilities housing vulnerable populations such as Long Term Care Facilities (Ltc), day cares, schools, underserved. populations and other segments of the populations where special considerations are needed for evacuating or sheltering in place. Maintains a list of hazardous materials response equipment in the County (Attachment C); and Performs emergency functions as denoted in the Basic Plan. In addition, performs the following hazardous materials-related functions: O Assists IC/uC with notification or make notification to state and federal or other affected entities; 0 Provides support to the IC/UC as needed; Initiates the joint information system (Jis) for the incident if needed; 0 Initiates the County TiCP if needed;. 0 Collects and evaluates situational awareness information and reports conditions to the Board of Supervisors, the AzsERC, and others, as appropriate; and 0 Activates the Emergency Operations Center (EOc) to support IC/uc for major incidents. 4.8 Cochise County Sheriff's Department The department performs emergency functions as denoted in the Basic Plan. In addition, performs the following. hazardous materials functions: -8

Maintains awareness level hazardous materials training for response personnel, Prepares policies and procedures for response to bioterrorism incidents, and . Coordinates with the CCEM for other on-scene or EOC needs. 4.11 Cochise County Public Works Department The department performs emergency functions as denoted in the cceop. In addition, performs the following hazardous materials functions:. Maintains awareness level hazardous materials training for response personnel, and Coordinates with the Sheriff's Department on any traffic control needs for the incident.. 9

5.1 Notifications Attachment D shows the general flow of notifications for suspected hazardous materials incidents. Notifications can be made in many ways, including from facilities or businesses, utilities, the public, or first responders. Initial notifications may require follow-up to additional assets after the initial incident assessment and as new information becomes available. Fixed facility owners/operators will immediately notify local law enforcement or fire district of hazardous materials incidents that originate at their facility that threaten public health, safety, property, or the environment. Owners/operators of vehicles involved in a hazardous material transportation incident will immediately notify the nearest dispatch by dialing 911 and informing them of hazardous materials involvement.. Incorporated cities/towns, upon receiving a report of a hazardous materials incident within its jurisdiction, will dispatch available responders to the scene and request additional assistance as required based on the call-taking guide and applicable SOPs/SOGs. If the incident involves an RQ of a hazardous substance as defined under CERCLA 103(a), the owner/operator will immediately notify the NRC (1-800-424-8802). They should also notify AZDEQ/AZSERC (1-800-234-5677 / 602-390- 7894). Initial reports should be submitted to the jurisdictional dispatch as soon as possible after arrival on the scene and include as much information as possible on the material, container, release status, etc. Updated information should be submitted as circumstances allow. All hazardous materials incidents in the County's unincorporated areas will be reported to the Sheriff's Department and by the Sheriff's Department to CCEM. Any response agency receiving a report of a hazardous materials incident will:. Convey available information on the material, container, release, injuries, etc., to their dispatch;. Notify at-risk facilities (schools, hospitals, nursing homes, etc.) in the threatened area of the potential for sheltering in place or evacuation. At-risk facilities are in the Facility Emergency Response Plan or the fire department/district pre-plans;. Attempt to contact the RP. If the RP is unavailable or unreachable, the IC/Uc should notify the National Response Center, and then the RP can follow up with the required information; Report incidents on state routes or I-10 to AZDPS (602-223-2212) and AZD0T (800-379-3701); Notify AZDEQ/AzSERC (1-800-234-5677/602-390-7894) for all incidents involving shipments between the US and Mexico, a release that threatens Mexico, or if AzDEQ resources are required; Notify affected facility owners such as AzDoT, pipeline facilities (El Paso Gas, All American Pipeline, etc.), railroads (Union Pacific Transportation Corp., Arizona Eastern Railroad, or Ferrocarril Pacifico), or others such as the US Forest Service, or US Border Patrol, etc.;. Notify the Fort Huachuca Emergency Operations Center (520-533-2291) if the incident involves a military aircraft or munitions or affects their facilities; and. Notify the appropriate Mexican authorities if the incident affects Mexico:. oAgua Prieta, Sonora (011-526- 536-2125 or (011-526-534-3282) or o Naco, Sonora (011-526-536-2125 or (011-526-534-3282).

5.2 Incident Communications Cochise County maintains a mutual aid frequency available to emergency responders in the county. The County maintains a Tactical Interoperable Communications Plan (TicP) to aid emergency responders when responding to incidents within the confines of Cochise County. The county also has mutual aid and interagency frequencies available for use through the dispatch centers. Responding Agencies will use primary, day-to-day channels for most incidents. Agencies will follow the TicP to meet communications needs if the response grows beyond a routine incident.
- 11

6.1.1 Documentation In addition to the documentation required in the CCEOp, the IC/UC will develop an incident action plan (IAP) per OSHA 1910.120(q) and sOPs/sOGs. All response agencies will keep detailed chronological logs and other documentation of activities to assist with reimbursement and after-action reviews and to maintain a legal record of their activities. To provide citizens and governments with information concerning potential chemical hazards in their communities, the CCLEPC will make Tier IIl general information on the hazardous materials in the county, reports of chemical releases, and post-incident reports available through information requests through AZsERC. The AZsERC Coordinator will fulfill the request per the State's Tier Il / FOIA policy. Requests are to be site-specific. The AzsERc Tier Il database will contain secure document files for which a facility has requested trade secret status as provided in Section 322 of EPCRA. 6.1.2 After-Action Report For significant events, the CCEM, the department having jurisdiction, or the facility should convene an after-action. review of the incident. From this meeting, a report containing after-actions and opportunities for improvement will be developed to help guide improvements in procedures, resources, documentation, training, and exercise opportunities. The report should be given to AzsERc or the cCLEPc for documentation and improvement opportunities.

6.2 Finance The response agencies may submit requests for reimbursement through RP insurers according to their jurisdiction's policies. If the RP is unknown or refuses to accept responsibility and there is an immediate threat to public safety and health, the state on-scene coordinator (sosc) will initiate action to effect cleanup using State or Federal funding. sources. Any additional or unrecoverable expenses will be submitted through the Cochise County Emergency Management Office for submission to state or federal agencies as appropriate. 6.3 Logistics Resource requests will be handled on-scene by the IC/UC and NIMS protocols initially. Request for assistance through. AZMAC or state resources should be made through CCEM unless those agencies already have jurisdictional overlap.
- 12

7.1 Plan Development and Maintenance This Plan will be reviewed, updated, and readopted at least annually. However, the Plan can be updated at any time if there is information of significance to add or change. Any recommended changes should be forwarded to the CCLEPC via Cochise County Emergency Management at 1415 Melody Lane, Building A, Bisbee, Arizona 85603 (520- 432-9220). Each change should be recorded on the Record of Changes. Each incorporated area, fire department/district, and the County is responsible for determining what level of training and resources are necessary for hazardous materials operations within its jurisdiction. The Cochise County Emergency Management maintains a listing of Cochise County emergency resources. An HsEeP-compliant hazardous materials incident tabletop, functional, or full-scale exercise will be conducted at least once every two years and coordinated through the CCLEPC and the CCEM. Exercises will be designed to meet the response needs for hazardous materials manufactured, stored, or transported through the county. Exercises should build upon current capabilities to improve plans, organizational structures, equipment, training, and exercise improvement and include whole community response partners, including private, federal, state, and local assets. Each exercise will have an After Action Report/Improvement Plan (AAR/IP) developed and presented to the CCLEPC. 8.2 Definitions Arizona State Emergency Response Commission (AzsERc): A State of Arizona Commission established by the Thirty- eighth Arizona Legislature under requirements of SARA, Title IIl. It includes representation from the Arizona Departments of Environmental Quality, Health Services, Public Safety, and Transportation and is chaired by the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality Director. The Commission deals with issues that include technical support and training, development of appropriate legislation, review of local plans, and establishment of community right-to-know information systems. Arizona Department of Emergency and Military Affairs/Emergency Management (AzDEMA/EM): This state office coordinates statewide preparedness and response to all natural and technological disasters, including hazardous materials releases. They provide training for Arizona first responders for all levels of hazardous materials responders through DOT grants administered by AZDEQ. Cochise County Emergency Operations Plan (cceop): Multi-hazard, functional plan that treats emergency management activities generally. It describes the emergency organization and the means of coordination with other entities. Contain Base Plan, Appendices, ESFs, and Annexes. Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act (CERCLA) of 1980 (42 UsC 9601 et seq.): Provides federal authority to respond to releases or threatened releases of hazardous substances and pollutants or contaminants that may endanger public health, welfare, or the environment. Commonly referred to as "Superfund". Community Coordinator: The person designated to maintain and implement this Plan according to EPcRA 303. For Cochise County, this is the Cochise County Emergency Management's Director or their designee.
- 14 -

Community Lifelines: A lifeline enables the continuous operation of critical government and business functions and
is essential to human health and safety or economic security. Critical lifelines for a community include safety and
security; food, hydration, and shelter; health and medical; energy; communications; transportation; hazardous
materials; and water systems.
Emergency and Hazardous Chemical Inventory (Tier Il) Reports: Chemical-specific reports must be submitted under
Section 312 of SARA, Title Ill. Reports are due by March 1st each year for the previous calendar year. All facilities
with EHS and hazardous chemicals above established thresholds must submit these reports.
Extremely Hazardous Substance (EHs): A substance on the list described in Section 302(a) (2) of SARA, Title III
and published in 40 CFR 355, Appendices A & B. This list currently contains more than 300 chemicals. Because
of their extremely toxic properties, these chemicals were chosen to provide an initial focus for chemical
emergency planning. If these chemicals are released in certain amounts, they may be of immediate concern to
the community. Releases of EHS must be reported immediately.
Facility: All buildings, structures, and other stationary items located on a single site or contiguous or adjacent
sites owned or operated by the same person. The term includes motor vehicles, rolling stock, pipelines, and
aircraft for emergency planning.
Facility Response Coordinator (FRc): A designated representative from each facility with an EHS in a quantity
exceeding its TPQ who participates in the emergency planning process.
Hazard Analysis: The procedure for identifying potential sources of a hazardous materials release, determining an
area's vulnerability to such a release, and comparing hazards to determine the overall risk to a community.
Hazard Identification: Provides information as to which facilities have extremely hazardous substances (EHS),
what they are, how much there is, and how it is stored at each facility. Mandatory facility reporting under SARA,
Title Ill provides most of the information needed. Also, the process responders use to identify and verify the
hazardous materials released and vulnerabilities based on the current situation.
Hazardous Chemical (OshA): Hazardous chemical means any chemical that is classified as a physical hazard or a
health hazard, a simple asphyxiant, combustible dust, pyrophoric gas, or hazard not otherwise classified.
Hazardous Materials (AzDEQ): It includes any hazardous materials designated by the DOT; any element, compound,
mixture, solution, or substance designated under CERcLA; any substance designated in EPcRA; any substance
designated FwPCA or CWA; any hazardous waste having the characteristics identified under or listed under Arizona
hazardous waste rules; any imminently hazardous chemical substance or mixture with respect to which action is
taken the TscA; any material or substance determined to be radioactive under the Atomic Energy Act; any substance
designated as a hazardous substance under Arizona "water Quality" definitions; and any hazardous chemical or
regulated substance as listed in the CAA (ASL, 2009s).
Hazardous Materials (Dot): A substance or material that the Secretary of Transportation has determined is capable
of posing an unreasonable risk to health, safety, and property when transported in commerce, which includes
hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, marine pollutants, elevated temperature materials, and any other
materials meeting any of the nine hazard classes.
Hazardous Substances (EPA): In general, the term means any substance designated pursuant to section 311 of the
Clean Water Act (CwA), any element, compound, mixture, solution, or substance designated pursuant to CERcLA,
any hazardous waste having the characteristics identified under or listed pursuant RcRA, any toxic pollutant listed
under the CwA, any hazardous air pollutant listed under the CAA, and any imminently hazardous chemical substance
or mixture with respect to which the Administrator has taken action pursuant to section of TscA. The term does not
include petroleum, natural gas, natural gas liquids, liquefied natural gas, or synthetic gas usable for fuel (petroleum
exclusion).
Hazardous Substance (OshA): Any substance listed by the DOT as a hazardous material under 49 CFR 172.101
15

Hazardous Waste (EPA and AzDeQ): This is a solid waste that is not excluded and is either a listed or a characteristic
waste.
Incident Commander (ic): The individual responsible for managing the tactical field operations of all involved
departments and supporting agencies/organizations at or near the scene of an emergency.
Incident Command System (ics): A nationally recognized system of managing small to large-scale events
through the systematic development of a complete, functional organization. The system uses common
terminology, is modular, and has a unified command structure, manageable span of control, consolidated
action plans, comprehensive resource management, and integrated communications.
Joint Information Center (Jic): A physical facility where all incident-related public information activities occur. It is
the central point of contact for all news media at the incident scene. Public information officials from all participating
agencies should collocate at the JIC.
Joint Information System (Jis): A process where all responding agencies, political jurisdictions, and elected officials
collaborate on the messaging to be shared with the public. Joint press releases will be issued through this system,
and when necessary, the JIS will be the mechanism used to deliver joint press conferences.
Local Emergency Planning Committee (LEPC): A committee formed under provisions of SARA Title III 301 to
implement local government compliance with the act..
Safety Data Sheets (sDs): Technical information required under the OsHA Hazard Communications Standard
describing toxicity, physical hazards, and safe handling methods for hazardous chemicals. Formerly known as
Material Safety Data Sheets..
National Response Center (Nrc): is a part of the federally established National Response System and is
staffed 24 hours a day by the Us Coast Guard. It is the designated federal point of contact for reporting all
oil, chemical, radiological, biological, and etiological discharges into the environment anywhere in the United
States and its territories. The Center receives and disseminates notices of discharges or releases to the
appropriate federal On-Scene Coordinator. The toll-free number, 1- 800-424-8802, can be reached 24 hours
a day for reporting actual or potential pollution accidents..
OsHA Hazard Communications Standard: 29 CFR 1910.1200, which requires communicating risks from
hazardous chemicals to workers in regulated facilities..
OSHA Hazardous Waste Operations and Emergency Response Standards (HAzwOPER): 29 CFR 1910.120
covers workers during emergency response to hazardous materials and requirements for handling hazardous
waste in the workplace.
Person: Any individual, trust, firm, joint stock company, corporation, partnership, association, State,
municipality, commission, political subdivision of a State, or interstate body.
Radioactive Material: Material that emits radiation (subatomic particles or pure energy) in the process of
stabilization.
Release: Any spilling, leaking, pumping, pouring, emitting, emptying, discharging, injecting, escaping, leaching,
dumping, or disposing into the environment (including the abandonment or discarding of barrels, containers, and
other receptacles) of any hazardous chemical..
Reportable Quantity (RQ): Designated amounts under CERCLA 102 for certain hazardous substances or EPCRA
302 for certain extremely hazardous substances that must be reported when released. If a substance is released in
a quantity equal to or exceeding its RQ, it must be immediately reported per EPCRA S 304, ARs 49-128, and CERCLA
103(a).
Response: The efforts to minimize the risks created in an emergency by protecting the people, the environment,
and property and the efforts to return the scene to normal pre-emergency conditions.
- 16 -
